title: Repairing lifting veneer on a table top
subtitle: Re-glue a delaminating veneer bubble or raised edge without removing the surrounding finish
excerpt: How to repair lifting or blistered veneer on a table top: assessing the extent of the separation, removing old failed adhesive, injecting or applying fresh hide glue or PVA, pressing the veneer flat under cauls, and blending the repaired area back into the surrounding surface.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: must be pressed flat with a

and cramps. A hot iron on its own can wake up old hide glue, but the bond will not last if the old glue has gone off. Decide first whether the blister can be pressed flat without cutting it open. A small blister with whole edges can often be re-glued through the veneer itself.

Press the blister gently and release. If it springs back, the veneer is intact and elastic; the bond has failed but the veneer can be re-glued without cutting. If the blister is cracked or has hardened in the raised position, it will need to be slit and the veneer eased flat before re-gluing. Check the surrounding veneer for further delamination by running a finger across the surface: a hollow-sounding tap under the knuckle indicates further separation beyond the visible blister edge.
Step 2, slit and clean out old adhesive

For a blister that will not press flat: use a sharp utility knife to cut a single fine slit along the grain through the centre of the blister. Work the knife blade gently under the veneer edge to open the blister slightly. With a thin filler knife, scrape out as much old glue residue as possible from both the underside of the veneer and the substrate face. On a hide-glue joint, apply a small amount of warm water to soften the remaining residue before scraping.

For a period piece originally hide-glued: use liquid fish glue (which has a longer open time than hot hide glue and does not need heating) or hot hide glue if the repair can be cramped within 45 seconds. For modern furniture with a PVA or urea-formaldehyde bond: use fresh PVA wood glue. Apply the glue sparingly to both the underside of the veneer and the substrate face through the slit. Too much glue causes squeeze-out that is hard to clean from the veneer surface.

Place a piece of waxed paper over the repair to prevent the caul sticking to any squeeze-out. Place a flat, smooth caul board (MDF or blockboard at least 18 mm thick) over the paper. Apply cramps over the caul, distributing pressure evenly across the repair area. Leave under pressure for at least 4 hours for hide glue or fish glue, and 12 hours for PVA. Remove the cramps and peel away the waxed paper.

If a slit was made, it will be visible as a fine line. Fill it with a wax stick matched to the veneer colour, applied by rubbing the stick across the slit then scraping flush with the edge of a credit card. If the repaired area is slightly lower or higher than the surrounding surface, sand carefully with 320-grit on a flat cork block, always working with the grain. Re-apply the finish to the repair area to match the surrounding sheen.

attrs.fix: Re-open the blister, scrape both surfaces to bare wood, and re-glue with fresh adhesive.
attrs.cause: The old adhesive residue was not fully removed before re-gluing; the new glue bonded to the old failed residue rather than to the wood.
attrs.symptom: Blister re-lifts within a few weeks of repair

attrs.fix: Dampen the veneer surface very lightly with a barely damp cloth and allow to sit for 2 minutes before pressing; moisture restores some flexibility. If the veneer is heavily cracked, the damaged area will need to be replaced with a patch cut from matching veneer.
attrs.cause: The veneer has dried out and become brittle, or the slit was made against the grain rather than along it.
